stories.title: GLP-1 Users Are Reshaping Retail With Every Pound They Shed
stories.excerpt: In 2024, according to a Gallup survey, just 3 percent of U.S. adults were using GLP-1 medications to lose weight. By July 2026, that share had soared to 11 percent, with 15 percent saying they had at some point tried a GLP-1 drug such as Ozempic or Wegovy for weight loss. This means that right now, around 1 in 8 American adults are making a serious attempt to eat healthier, live less destructive lifestyles, and transform into the idealized versions of themselves they had long been trying, but failing, to become. Knowing the traditional purchasing power of aspirational Americans, it seems unlikely that these changes would come without a corresponding shift in shopping habits that in turn would have a serious effect on retailers. The quest for a better, healthier self, after all, means specific lifestyle changes: choosing healthier food and smaller portions at grocery stores and restaurants, working out at the local gym, and finally being able to fit into that outfit that’s been whispering seductively to you from behind a retailer’s display window or website. It comes as little surprise, then, to learn that retailers are indeed seeing the GLP-1-inspired effects of this trend in both foot traffic and sales figures. A study in the Journal of Marketing Research published in December 2025 analyzed the spending of tens of thousands of U.S. households where a GLP-1 drug was being used for weight loss. The study found that households reduce grocery spending by an average of 5.3 percent within six months of starting the medication, with the spending of higher-income households falling 8 percent. Fast-food, coffee shop and other limited- service eatery spending also drops by 8 percent, while spending on snacks and sweets falls by 10 percent. An April 2026 report from consultancy McKinsey & Company delved deeper, breaking down sales drops by specific categories. Among the survey’s findings: Six months after starting a GLP-1, consumers spent 11.5 percent less on chips and ot
bullets: GLP-1 use jumped from 3% (2024) to 11% of U.S. adults by July 2026; 15% have tried drugs like Ozempic.
Weight loss drives smaller food portions, healthier eating, gym memberships, and discretionary apparel purchases.
Retail tenant demand shifting toward grocery, fitness, health-focused merchants; negative for traditional food service.
stories.summary: GLP-1 medication adoption has exploded to 11% of U.S. adults, triggering behavioral shifts toward healthier consumption, smaller portions, fitness, and lifestyle changes. This reshapes retail tenant mix and shopping patterns at multifamily-adjacent centers. Retailers report measurable changes in category performance as aspirational consumers remake their habits.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers should anticipate tenant shifts in ground-floor and nearby retail: growth in health/wellness, smaller-format grocery, fitness, and premium prepared-foods; decline in traditional QSR and casual dining. Mixed-use projects near affluent metros (LA, NYC, Miami, DC) face upside from fitness, wellness retail demand but downside from traditional restaurant anchors—requires flexible ground-floor planning and forward-looking TI agreements.

stories.title: Brookfield, CPP Taking LXP Industrial Private in $5.2B Deal
stories.excerpt: Brookfield Asset Management and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board have agreed to take LXP Industrial Trust private in an all-cash transaction valued at approximately $5.2 billion, including net debt and preferred equity. West Palm Beach-based LXP owns one of the largest portfolios of warehouse and logistics facilities in the U.S., comprising approximately 53 million square feet across 108 properties in the Sunbelt and Midwest. Founded as Lexington Realty Trust, LXP launched a repositioning strategy in 2018. “This transaction is the culmination of the LXP team’s successful execution of our strategic plan to transform LXP into a pure-play industrial REIT, curate a best-in-class portfolio, and implement our development program,” said Thomas W. Eglin, Jr., chairman and CEO. At Brookfield Real Estate, CEO Lowell Baron said, “LXP has assembled a high-quality industrial portfolio with modern logistics assets in attractive markets. The acquisition aligns with our strategy of investing in high-quality real estate with durable cash flows and opportunities to create value through active asset management.” The merger agreement includes a 40-day “go-shop” period expiring at 11:59 p.m. New York City time on August 28. Subject to the terms and conditions of the agreement, LXP may terminate the transaction and the definitive agreement to enter into a transaction that constitutes a superior proposal, subject to the payment of a termination fee. BofA Securities, Inc. is acting as lead financial advisor, J.P. Morgan Securities LLC is acting as co-financial advisor and Hogan Lovells Cadwalader US LLP is serving as legal advisor to LXP. Citigroup Global Markets Inc. and Morgan Stanley are serving as financial advisors and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP and Thompson Hine LLP are serving as legal advisors to Brookfield and CPP Investments, with DLA Piper LLP serving as legal advisor to CPP Investments in connection with certain aspects of the transaction. Dechert LLP is acting as lega
bullets: Brookfield & CPP taking LXP Industrial private at $5.2B; 53M SF across Sunbelt & Midwest warehouses.
Institutional mega-capital consolidating best-in-class logistics portfolios amid elevated cap rates, targeting long-term hold.
Industrial REIT transformation signals investor appetite for pure-play logistics; portfolio quality premium over occupancy cycles.
stories.summary: Brookfield Asset Management and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board are acquiring LXP Industrial Trust (53 million SF, 108 properties) for $5.2 billion, taking the Sunbelt and Midwest logistics REIT private. The deal reflects institutional capital's confidence in modern logistics fundamentals and long-term cash-flow stability. LXP's repositioning into a pure-play industrial operator made it an attractive takeout candidate.
stories.impact: Signals strong institutional appetite for stabilized, modern industrial product in Sunbelt logistics markets; cap rates remain competitive despite higher rates, favoring trophy assets. Multifamily developers should monitor industrial cap-rate compression and land value inflation in Sunbelt nodes (Atlanta, Charlotte, Nashville, Austin), which compete for site acquisition. Industrial hyperscaling by mega-capital may accelerate land scarcity and pricing in secondary, logistics-proximate markets.

stories.title: Investment Firm HarbourVest Partners Takes 20K SF at 9 West 57th Street
stories.excerpt: HarbourVest Partners, a global private markets investment firm, is moving into its first New York City office, Commercial Observer has learned. The firm, which is headquartered in Boston, has inked a 10-year deal for 19,752 square feet on part of the 49th floor of the Soloviev Group’s 9 West 57th Street. The asking rent was not disclosed, but Commercial Observer previously reported a record asking rent at the Midtown office tower — also known as the Solow Building — of $327.50 per square foot. The average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $98.98 per square foot during the first half of 2026, according to Colliers research. HarbourVest Partners was represented by JLL’s Alexander Chudnoff and Ava Loughlin, while Soloviev Group was represented by CBRE’s Howard Fiddle, John Maher, Gregg Rothkin, Tara Rhodes and Alex Leopold. CBRE did not immediately respond to a request for comment. “Today’s leading financial firms are increasingly focused on attracting and retaining top talent, and the office they choose has become a critical part of that strategy,” Chudnoff said in a statement. “HarbourVest wanted its office in New York City to reflect the caliber of its business, and to provide an exceptional experience for both clients and employees. Few buildings deliver that combination better than 9 West 57th Street, which continues to attract many of the world’s most prestigious financial and investment firms.” Built in 1974, 9 West 57th Street is a 49-story office building with tenants including oil and energy company Hess Group and global finance firm Tikehau Capital. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: HarbourVest Partners leases 19,752 SF at 9 West 57th Street, Midtown Manhattan, at record ~$327.50/SF.
Average Midtown office rent $98.98/SF (H1 2026); luxury trophy lease 3.3x market to retain top talent.
Private markets investor relocation signals continued flight to flagship trophy space for talent attraction.
stories.summary: HarbourVest Partners, a Boston-based private markets firm, signed a 10-year, 20K-SF lease at 9 West 57th Street in Midtown Manhattan at or near record asking rents (~$327.50/SF), roughly 3.3 times the Midtown average of $98.98/SF. The move reflects an industry trend of financial and investment firms paying premier rents to occupy flagship office to attract and retain talent.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, trophy office premiumization in Midtown signals white-collar wage growth and talent concentration in Manhattan, supporting underlying rental demand in luxury residential proxies (NoHo, Hudson Yards, Flatiron). The rent premium paid by elite financial firms suggests labor-market tightness and high compensation for senior talent—tailwind for Class-A multifamily in tech/finance-dense submarkets.

stories.title: Korean Grocery Sensation H Mart Wants to Conquer the Sun Belt
stories.excerpt: Shopping hauls are some of the Internet’s most popular videos, but some Gen Zers are sampling and sharing their impressions of an Asian supermarket’s products instead of their latest luxury splurge. In February, TikTok user Linda Vo (@itslindavo) posted a short clip of her emptying a paper bag of Korean salt bread, a triangle kimbap, vermicelli, pears and puffy rice cakes that she purchased at her local H Mart, a Lyndhurst, N.J.-based chain of grocery stores that specialize in Asian goods. “I went to H Mart because I’ve been seeing so many viral things online and I had to try them for myself,” she said. In April, TikTokerer Ari Kytsya (@notburnttoasthehe) filmed a haul where she slathered cooked baby octopus with Sriracha mayo, slurped on a yogurt drink in a package resembling a baby bottle, then bit into a purple piece of ice cream shaped like a blackberry. Her review for each item consisted of making an increasingly louder, longer “Mmmm!” sound. A month later, an influencer named Leo Baek described an array of Korean products, including carbonated milk sodas, Korean rice cakes, Korean pears and perilla leaves he piled into his shopping cart set to a chill, feel-good Anderson .Paak song. It may seem unusual for a grocery store, let alone a specialty market offering produce and products imported from East Asia, to attract so much social media attention. But H Mart has become a nimble operator in a highly competitive industry with $2 billion in annual sales and more than 100 locations across the U.S. and Canada. Now the chain is proceeding with a rapid expansion into some of the nation’s fastest-growing communities in Florida, Texas, California and Nevada. Along the way, H Mart became an essential destination for Asian American families seeking authentic ingredients from their homelands that couldn’t be found in other grocery stores, a bond celebrated in Michelle Zauner’s bestselling 2021 memoir Crying in H Mart. Soon a much broader audience of Americans, who have em
bullets: H Mart (Asian supermarket chain, Lyndhurst NJ) expanding aggressively into Sunbelt; Gen Z viral TikTok demand driver.
Asian retail tenant seeking new markets signals ethnic/demographic shift and e-commerce/social commerce tailwinds.
Growth anchors grocery-centered mixed-use; demand concentrated in metros with Asian population growth (DFW, Austin, Houston, Phoenix).
stories.summary: H Mart, a Korean/Asian specialty grocery chain, is pursuing expansion into Sunbelt markets, leveraging viral social media buzz and Gen Z interest in Asian products and culture. The chain is capitalizing on demographic growth, ethnic retail demand, and a trend of younger shoppers treating specialty grocery shopping as aspirational content.
stories.impact: For multifamily developers in Sunbelt metros (DFW, Austin, Houston, Phoenix, South Florida): H Mart expansion signals strong ethnic grocery demand and tenant diversity in mixed-use ground floors. Signals demographic pull toward Asian community clusters and younger, value-conscious, digitally-native renters. Consider H Mart-anchored or Asian-retail-heavy mixed-use sites in submarkets with growing Asian population (Irving TX, Austin NE, Phoenix Ahwatukee, Miami-Dade). Tenant diversification reduces reliance on traditional supermarket anchors.

stories.title: Dedeaux Properties CEO Brett Dedeaux On California’s Next Industrial Cycle
stories.excerpt: Few names are as engrained in Southern California industrial real estate — or baseball — as Dedeaux. The family business traces its roots to legendary University of Southern California baseball coach Rod Dedeaux, who led the Trojans to 11 national championships and coached U.S. Olympic teams after founding trucking company Dart in 1938, which eventually grew it into a nationwide logistics operation. Dart acquired industrial properties for its own use, and then started leasing space to customers. That evolved into what is now Los Angeles-based Dedeaux Properties, which develops and invests in logistics, distribution facilities and specialized industrial outdoor storage (IOS) properties. CEO Brett Dedeaux is steering the business through a far more complicated market. After years of soaring demand, Southern California is emerging from a period of elevated vacancy and tariff uncertainty. But Dedeaux said tenants have continued expansion plans while investors return to the scene. Commercial Observer caught up with Dedeaux to discuss where that recovery is taking hold, why advanced manufacturing and IOS are outperforming, and how rising power demands are changing the buildings his firm develops. The following is Part 1 of a two-part interview, and has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: At the start of the year, you said 2026 would be a transition from “reset to recovery” for industrial real estate. Are you seeing that play out, and do you expect that to continue in the second half of the year? A rendering of Dedeaux Properties’ Tejon Ranch industrial facility. RENDERING: Courtesy Dedeaux Properties Brett Dedeaux: There have been terms used since the beginning of the year by some of our institutional capital partners, who have a pretty good holistic viewpoint because they own a lot of industrial here in Southern California and other asset types. One phrase I have heard is “bouncing along the bottom.” I do not love the term, but it is a phrase t
bullets: SoCal industrial emerging from elevated vacancy, tariff uncertainty; Dedeaux CEO reports tenant expansion ongoing despite headwinds.
Advanced manufacturing and industrial outdoor storage (IOS) outperforming logistics; investor activity returning to LA market.
Industrial cycle recovery sign: tenants expanding, cap rates stabilizing, build-to-suit demand active in logistics corridors.
stories.summary: Brett Dedeaux, CEO of Los Angeles-based Dedeaux Properties, notes that Southern California industrial is recovering from a period of elevated vacancy and tariff-driven caution. Despite headwinds, tenants are advancing expansion plans and investors are re-entering the market. Advanced manufacturing and specialized industrial outdoor storage are notably outperforming traditional distribution.
stories.impact: Southern California industrial recovery will tighten logistics land supply and push land costs upward, especially in premium IE and LA County nodes. Multifamily developers competing for land in Inland Empire, Ontario, Long Beach, and LA Harbor should expect accelerating industrial bid-up, especially for build-to-suit sites near Class-A warehouses. The strength in advanced manufacturing and IOS (higher-margin asset classes) suggests industrial capital is rotating away from garden-variety logistics—favoring specialized, higher-yielding product and creating pockets of supply scarcity that could inflate land values.

stories.title: Sunday Summary: Don’t Feel Embarrassed.
stories.excerpt: We’ve all had awkward moments. Disagreements at work. Faux pas in formal settings. The odd harmless fib that spins out of control. In the commercial real estate trade right now, the most awkward moments might be coming via a new trend that we covered this past week: owners such as RXR, SL Green, Ares Management and others launching special servicing platforms. These platforms theoretically give owners windows into the proprietary goings-on of their competitors’ distressed assets. Some, such as Fitch Ratings, see “the potential for conflicts of interest” — the proverbial foxes guarding the henhouse. Others see no awkwardness at all. “The opportunity is there and the perception is there for conflict, but I have not seen any evidence of a transaction that resulted in lower proceeds for the bondholder because an affiliate picked it up,” Neil Shapiro, partner in the real estate practice at New York City law firm Herrick Feinstein, told us. “There’s a lot of positive impacts with companies that have real estate development expertise being able to use that to help on a real estate workout.” Besides, the law’s the law, starting with the ratings agencies that decide a property’s distress level. “Their entire rating agency business is based on the upholding of securities law,” said a source familiar with the special servicing process. “That is why they have ratings, and special servicers have to abide by the special servicing standard to maintain those ratings. So I think there is a pretty tight control on material non-public information.” REIT to the point Here’s another potentially awkward situation. You’re a real estate investment trust doing dynamite business — strong (even historic) leasing, consistent debt service, brisk trades of underperforming assets — but your stock just doesn’t reflect the pace. That’s the case for a lot of REITs in different sectors, especially office. What’s causing the disconnect? We went deep with some of the biggest REIT experts to find the an
bullets: Special servicing platforms by RXR, SL Green, Ares create potential conflicts of interest in distressed asset sales.
Ratings agencies (Fitch) flag foxes-guarding-henhouse risk; no evidence yet of bondholder-harming abuse, per counsel.
Trend signals CMBS distress persisting; developer-operators leveraging expertise in workouts, creating 'grey zone' opportunities.
stories.summary: Major real estate owners (RXR, SL Green, Ares) are launching special servicing platforms that grant visibility into competitors' distressed assets, creating potential conflicts of interest. Fitch Ratings flagged governance risks, though counsel notes no evidence yet of misconduct. The trend underscores ongoing CMBS distress and developer-operators' efforts to position themselves as workout specialists.
stories.impact: Signals continued CMBS/loan market stress and prolonged distress cycle. For multifamily developers with balance sheets and development expertise, distressed-asset-purchase opportunities may persist in office-to-multifamily conversion and mixed-use redevelopment—especially in secondary markets with soft office demand. Expect extended hold periods and workout timelines on troubled multifamily assets; cap-rate compression may be delayed. Regulatory clarity on special servicing conflicts could also reshape loan-sale and acquisition landscape.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan & Gounder ordered to pay $400M (incl. $300M punitive) for fake San Gabriel River development fraud.
Pair had prior SEC allegations of $130M Ponzi scheme targeting DFW Indian-American community; pattern of fraud.
Fake development marketing showed canal, parks, retail on 400 acres; zero intent to build; contractor Hussien lost home.
stories.summary: Two Frisco real estate developers, Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder, were found liable for $400 million in damages (including $300 million in punitive damages) for fraudulently marketing a 400-acre San Gabriel River mixed-use development they never intended to build. The pair had prior SEC allegations of a $130 million Ponzi scheme targeting DFW's Indian-American community. The fraud duped Energy Commissioning Inc. into $21 million of infrastructure work.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily market impact; primarily a cautionary tale on developer credibility and fraud risk in speculative land development. For institutional capital and lenders in DFW and Central Texas, highlights need for rigorous developer vetting, third-party verification of intent and feasibility, and escrow controls. May increase scrutiny on pre-development and capital-raise transparency in speculative mixed-use projects, particularly those targeting ethnic communities or diaspora investor bases. No broader supply or demand signal.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revises Marina Safeway (SF waterfront) from 790 to 848 units; reduces height 40 ft (25→22 stories).
Density optimization: fewer amenities, smaller ground-floor retail, modest grocery expansion (67.2K SF); 989.5K SF total.
Mayor Lurie opposes even revised plan; AB 2011 streamlining allows taller building; project remains contentious, Planning Dept. reviewing.
stories.summary: Align Real Estate redesigned its controversial 15 Marina Boulevard (Marina Safeway) project to increase units from 790 to 848 while cutting height by 40 and 23 feet on two towers (from 25/22 to 22/18 stories). The density play trades amenity and retail space for apartments, optimizing development economics under state law AB 2011. Mayor Lurie remains opposed; Planning Department continues review.
stories.impact: SF multifamily density-optimization trend signals state approval streamlines can push developers toward unit-count maximization over amenity/retail richness, especially on prime commercial land. The revision suggests developer confidence in market absorption of smaller units and limited amenity expectations. For developers in CA cities with AB 2011-eligible sites (commercial zoning near transit), expect upward unit-count pressure and tighter floor plans. Contentious approvals in SF may delay projects 1–2 years; coastal CA regulatory risk remains high despite state preemption. No supply relief near-term; political opposition persists.

stories.title: Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky
stories.excerpt: Colliers completed the sale of Gray Industrial Center Building 2, a newly completed 302,400-square-foot industrial facility at 530 Logistics Drive in Shelbyville, Kentucky, approximately 30 miles east of Louisville. The build-to-suit distribution facility is fully leased to Kuehne+Nagel, a global logistics provider. Colliers Industrial Capital Markets advisors Alex Cantu, Alex Davenport, Jeff Devine, Steve Disse and Tyler Ziebel, together with local CBRE leasing advisors Doug Butcher and Alex Grove, represented Gray Development. The property was sold to ElmTree Funds in a forward-funded acquisition that closed upon completion of the facility. “Gray Industrial Center Building 2 demonstrates the continued demand for build-to-suit industrial facilities in strategically located markets like Shelbyville, where access to the I-64 corridor and the greater Louisville region continues to attract both occupiers and institutional capital,” said Alex Cantu. “Forward-funded acquisitions of newly completed, fully leased assets remain highly attractive to investors seeking modern industrial product supported by long-term tenancy and strong market fundamentals.” Situated on approximately 32.19 acres, the Gray Industrial Center Building is located approximately one mile from Interstate 64. The property provides convenient access to Louisville International Airport and UPS Worldport. The post Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Colliers sold 302.4K-SF build-to-suit logistics facility (Gray Industrial Center Building 2, Shelbyville KY) fully leased to Kuehne+Nagel.
ElmTree Funds acquired via forward-funded deal upon completion; 32.19 acres, 1 mile from I-64, near Louisville airport, UPS Worldport.
Build-to-suit industrial asset fully leased at completion; institutional buyer (ElmTree) prefers modern, long-term-lease product for stability.
stories.summary: Colliers completed the sale of a newly finished 302,400-SF build-to-suit industrial distribution facility in Shelbyville, Kentucky (30 miles east of Louisville), fully leased to Kuehne+Nagel. ElmTree Funds acquired the asset via forward-funded acquisition upon completion on 32.19 acres near I-64 and Louisville International Airport. The transaction underscores institutional appetite for modern, fully-leased logistics assets in strategic secondary markets.
stories.impact: Shelbyville and Louisville logistics market remain strong for build-to-suit; tenant demand (Kuehne+Nagel) and capital continue to flow to I-64 corridor nodes. For multifamily developers in Louisville MSA, logistics strength may inflate land costs in industrial parks and submarket corridors (Shelbyville, Oldham County); suburban infill multifamily near logistics hubs may face land-cost headwinds. Secondary-market build-to-suit success suggests long-term leasing appetite and stable industrial fundamentals, but no direct multifamily demand signal.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced the sale of a net-leased restaurant property occupied by Storming Crab in Rockford, Illinois.“This offering generated significant investor interest due to the property’s strong operating performance, prime location and the limited supply of comparable retail assets in the market,” said Brian Parmacek, managing director of investments. “The asset received multiple offers above the asking price, ultimately attracting a private investor focused on securing long-term cash flow and income generation.”Parmacek, investment specialist in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Downtown office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, RCJD Canyon Crest, LLC, and procured the buyer, MBOGO Properties, LLC. The 5,860-square-foot freestanding property is located at 6779 E. State St. and is occupied by Storming Crab, a chain of casual restaurants specializing in Cajun-style seafood boils. The restaurant is surrounded by national retailers, including Target, ALDI, Ulta Beauty, Hobby Lobby, Burlington and PetSmart and is less than a half-mile from Hard Rock Casino Rockford. The post Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap sold net-leased Storming Crab restaurant (5,860 SF) in Rockford, IL; multiple offers above asking.
Location: 6779 E. State St., near Target, ALDI, Ulta, Hobby Lobby, <0.5 mi from Hard Rock Casino Rockford.
Strong investor demand for long-term lease, stable cash flow; limited supply of comparable retail assets in market.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of a 5,860-SF net-leased Storming Crab restaurant in Rockford, Illinois to a private investor seeking long-term cash flow. The property generated multiple offers above asking, indicating strong demand for stabilized retail leases in secondary markets. Tenant (Cajun-style seafood casual dining) is surrounded by national retailers and located near Hard Rock Casino Rockford.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily impact. Signals strong investor appetite for net-lease retail and restaurant assets in secondary markets (Rockford) near anchors (casinos, big-box retail). For multifamily developers with ground-floor retail in secondary metros, net-lease anchor tenants (QSR, grocery) remain attractive to institutional capital; but limited supply of comparable retail may inflate ground-floor lease rates or reduce TI availability. No demand or supply signal for multifamily itself.

stories.title: Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap finalized the sale of 312 Bowery, a multifamily property in Manhattan’s NoHo neighborhood, for $16.65 million. John Stewart and Dylan Torey of Marcus & Millichap exclusively marketed the property on behalf of the seller, Bremen House, Inc., and procured the buyer, Allied Realty & Development Corp. “Buyer demand was some of the strongest we’ve seen this year due to the scarcity of truly free-market multifamily properties,” said Stewart. “Well-maintained properties in core locations, with no historical rent regulation or with attorney-approved deregulation documentation, will continue to attract the most engaged buyers, with bidding wars and, in some cases, sub-five-cap final pricing.” The five-story, 23,000-square-foot building in the NoHo East historic district was built in 1920 and converted to multifamily in 2004. It includes 22 modernized free-market apartments. The property has approximately 7,000 square feet of unused development rights and a 3,900-square-foot ground-floor commercial space occupied by The Hole, an art gallery. Where the apartment industry connects. Meet the owners, investors, developers, lenders, brokers, and service providers driving today’s multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Build relationships, uncover opportunities, and stay ahead of what’s next. The post Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap sold 312 Bowery (Manhattan NoHo) 22-unit free-market multifamily for $16.65M; strong buyer demand.
Five-story, 23K SF, 1920 vintage, 2004 multifamily conversion; 7K SF unused air rights; 3.9K SF ground-floor art gallery.
Buyer demand 'strongest this year' for free-market, deregulated apartments; sub-five-cap pricing; sub-$900K/unit average.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap closed the $16.65 million sale of 312 Bowery, a 22-unit free-market multifamily property in Manhattan's NoHo East historic district, to Allied Realty & Development Corp. Broker reports 'some of the strongest demand this year' due to scarcity of truly free-market apartments with no rent regulation. Properties with deregulation documentation and core locations are attracting bidding wars and sub-five-cap pricing.
stories.impact: Critical Manhattan signal: free-market, deregulated multifamily remains scarce and highly sought by institutional and private capital; cap rates in Manhattan compress to sub-5% for quality, free-market assets (implying ~22%+ initial yields premium demanded). For developers in NY, NJ, and rent-regulated metros, regulatory risk discount is acute—buyers will pay steep premiums for documented deregulation or no-reg product. The 7K SF of unused air rights signal density upside and developer interest in further conversion or redevelopment. Shortage of free-market inventory in core markets (NoHo, LES, East Village) supports continued price appreciation and rental growth for unregulated buildings; rent-regulation risk limits capital availability.

stories.title: Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison
stories.excerpt: Nicholas & Associates, the construction management and general contracting arm of the Nicholas Family of Companies, has been selected by Moyer Properties as the general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury rental community set on a 4.86-acre site in Madison, Wisconsin. The ground-up development is the latest in Nicholas & Associates’ Wisconsin portfolio, further expanding the firm’s presence in the state and region. Designed by HKM Architects + Planners, Inc., Aster Flats will offer studio, one-, two- and three-bedroom units ranging in size from 588 to 1,395 square feet. “Being selected to build Aster Flats reflects the trust and strong reputation we’ve established in southeast Wisconsin through a series of high-profile projects in recent years,” said Nick Papnicholas Jr., chief executive officer and managing director of Nicholas Family of Companies. Nicholas & Associates will break ground in August on the new development, with construction happening in phases across the three buildings. Completion is anticipated in February 2028. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nicholas & Associates GC for Aster Flats (Madison WI): 263 units, 3 buildings, 4.86 acres, luxury rental, $5M+ construction budget.
Ground-up start August 2026; phased completion Feb 2028; studios to 3-BR, 588–1,395 SF; Moyer Properties developer.
HKM Architects design; Midwest portfolio expansion for Nicholas Family; strong contractor-developer partnerships in Wisconsin.
stories.summary: Nicholas & Associates has been selected as general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury multifamily development in Madison, Wisconsin, on 4.86 acres. The project breaks ground in August 2026 with phased completion by February 2028. Units range from studios to three-bedroom (588–1,395 SF), designed by HKM Architects.
stories.impact: Madison multifamily pipeline active; luxury ground-up construction remains viable in Midwest secondary markets with strong fundamentals (university town, employer base, tech inflow). For developers in Wisconsin and upper Midwest, construction labor and material costs remain moderate relative to coastal markets; project timelines (18 months, phased) are manageable. Aster Flats signals continued institutional developer confidence in Madison rental market; no indication of supply constraints or cost shocks. Wisconsin market likely remains softer than Sunbelt on rents/cap rates—good for development feasibility, less competitive for acquisition.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Urban Development Review Board approved OKO Lilli (53-story, 117-unit condo, Edgewater) & 619 Brickell by Nobu (75-story, 321-unit).
OKO Lilli: half-acre, Biscayne Bay frontage, 350-ft baywalk, ground-floor retail, 244-space garage at separate lot.
619 Brickell by Nobu: 2 acres, Brickell Ave, 1–5BR units, private elevator per unit; First Presbyterian Church parking lot site.
stories.summary: Miami's Urban Development Review Board approved two luxury residential towers: OKO Lilli (53-story, 117 condos, Edgewater, Biscayne Bay frontage) by Vlad Doronin's OKO Group, and 619 Brickell by Nobu (75-story, 321 condos, Brickell) by 13th Floor Investments. Both projects received favorable design reviews. OKO Lilli includes public baywalk and retail; Nobu tower includes high-end finishes and private elevator arrivals.
stories.impact: Miami luxury condo market remains robust; waterfront and branded luxury (Nobu) projects advancing swiftly through approvals. For multifamily developers competing in Miami, condo-conversion upside is available in Brickell, Edgewater, and Wynwood; however, branded luxury and foreign capital (Doronin) are capturing prime waterfront and high-visibility sites. Rental multifamily developers should focus on workforce/affordable + attainable segments (per Live Local Act tailwinds) rather than luxury condo competition. Both projects' UDRB approvals signal strong design-review support; no supply constraints for institutional capital.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners rejected data center zoning (Project Tango): 5-1 vote to deny 1M SF data center + 2.3M SF warehouses.
PBA Holdings, joint venture (Palm Beach Aggregates, Phillips Inc., Klein, Ogden Cap), owned 130 of 200 acres; significant community opposition.
Data center nixed on infrastructure, community impact concerns; warehouse approval (2M SF) already secured; resubmission allowed.
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny a zoning application for Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), a proposed 1 million-SF data center and 2.3 million-SF warehouse complex on ~200 acres in unincorporated County. Community and infrastructure concerns drove the denial. PBA Holdings (joint venture) owns ~130 acres and has already secured approval for 2 million SF of warehouses; data center denial is without prejudice, allowing resubmission.
stories.impact: Setback for South Florida data center development; regulatory risk and community resistance significantly delaying industrial expansion in Palm Beach County. Warehouse approval standing suggests logistics/distribution remain politically acceptable; hyperscale data center pull faces environmental and infrastructure headwinds. For multifamily developers in Palm Beach, logistics land scarcity may intensify as data-center zoning challenges reduce industrial supply. No direct multifamily impact, but industrial scarcity may inflate multifamily land costs in adjacent markets (Broward, Miami-Dade with less zoning friction).

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Musk-affiliated Wit Tech planning $5.2M renovation of Seaholm Power Plant office (35K SF, 800 W Cesar Chavez, Downtown Austin).
Musk companies occupy 35K SF; broader Austin empire tripled Bastrop land holdings; Terafab semiconductor fab in Grimes (55B projected, 6K+ acres).
Austin office capex signals HQ consolidation and long-term commitment; data center + manufacturing cluster emerging in Austin region.
stories.summary: Wit Tech, a SpaceX-linked company, is undertaking a $5.2 million renovation of 35,000 SF at the Seaholm Power Plant in Downtown Austin, where multiple Musk-affiliated companies have offices. The move reflects Musk's broader Austin expansion, including the $55 billion Terafab semiconductor manufacturing project in Grimes County and tripled land holdings in Bastrop. Musk has not publicly commented but infrastructure investments suggest long-term commitment.
stories.impact: Austin office market and broader Central Texas multifamily demand receiving major tailwind from Musk-company consolidation and Terafab cluster development. Downtown Austin office renovation (Seaholm) signals HQ/white-collar job creation; Grimes semiconductor fab and data-center expansions will pull blue-collar workers and support services. For multifamily developers in Austin (central, East Austin), expect sustained white-collar rental demand; for Bryan-College Station, Grimes, Bastrop, anticipate worker-housing demand (workforce/attainable multifamily) from semiconductor/manufacturing employment. Tax incentives and TIRZ funding in Grimes may create development-friendly conditions. Long regulatory timelines on Terafab (environmental, local resistance) may delay job materialization 2–3 years; housing demand likely lags project starts.

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pablo Castro (Spanish developer, first U.S. project) secured approval for HueHub: 4,040 units, 7×34-story towers, 12 acres, West Little River (Miami-Dade).
Live Local Act project: 100% furnished, attainably priced, all amenities free to residents; exceeds 40% workforce-housing threshold.
Castro sued by 3 local industry players (Tauber, others) alleging partnership breach, non-payment, title/financing disputes; legal risk high.
stories.summary: Spanish developer Pablo Castro has secured approval for HueHub, Florida's largest known multifamily project under the Live Local Act: 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River, Miami-Dade. All units are furnished and attainably priced (exceeding the 40% affordable requirement); amenities (gyms, learning centers) are free to residents. However, Castro faces three lawsuits from local developers alleging partnership breaches and non-payment; litigation has raised legal and title questions.
stories.impact: HueHub represents major Live Local Act upside in South Florida: density beyond traditional zoning (34 stories), scale (4K units), affordability incentive without density trade-off, and attainable pricing create model for workforce-multifamily developers. However, Castro's legal disputes (partnership claims, title clouds, non-payment allegations) signal execution risk and financial transparency concerns; institutional capital may demand robust bonding, escrow, and third-party oversight. Live Local streamlining has reduced approval timelines but increased litigation risk and developer-vetting demands. For competitors in South Florida, HueHub's approval proves Live Local feasibility; litigation outcomes will shape capital availability and deal structuring for future mega-projects.
